jokes.text: I've been searching for 25 minutes and I haven't found a single egg. And I'm an adult. Oh. Yeah, I forgot to do that.
jokes.setup: Leslie explains how exceptional Tom's Easter egg hiding job is, followed by her inability to find any eggs despite being an adult
jokes.punchline: Tom casually reveals he forgot to hide the eggs at all
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup-punchline structure. Leslie's earnest praise makes Tom's casual admission even funnier. Economy is excellent - maximum comedy from minimal words.

jokes.text: She's as respected as Mother Teresa. She's as powerful as Stalin, and she's as beautiful as Margaret Thatcher.
jokes.setup: Leslie wants to explain how wonderful her mother is using flattering comparisons
jokes.punchline: Margaret Thatcher as a beauty standard is absurd
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie character moment - her sincere attempt at compliments reveals her obliviousness. The escalation from respect to power to questionable beauty standard is well-crafted.

jokes.text: I know! That's why I fake invited you.
jokes.setup: Leslie's mom says she's too busy to attend Leslie's meeting
jokes.punchline: Leslie reveals she was fake inviting her anyway
jokes.explanation: Leslie's defensive mechanism of pretending rejection doesn't hurt. Shows her character's vulnerability disguised as bravado.

jokes.text: When I was in sixth grade, I was voted Best Dressed by 87 votes. And there were only 63 people in my class.
jokes.setup: Leslie explains her expertise in campaigning with a childhood example
jokes.punchline: She got more votes than voters existed
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie mathematical impossibility delivered with complete sincerity. Perfect economy and structure - sets up normal achievement, then reveals absurd details.

jokes.text: Tom, you probably won't need any [sunscreen for your beaks].
jokes.setup: Leslie offers sunscreen for everyone's 'beaks' (noses)
jokes.punchline: Tom won't need any because of his darker skin tone
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about sun protection and skin tone. Functional but not particularly clever or memorable.

jokes.text: You can't handle the pit. That's why we need to turn it into a park.
jokes.setup: Leslie's canvassing guide includes celebrity-specific pitches
jokes.punchline: A Few Good Men reference adapted to their pit situation
jokes.explanation: Clever adaptation of famous movie quote to their situation. Shows Leslie's over-preparation and pop culture awareness.

jokes.text: Would it change your mind if we told you that... She's gone.
jokes.setup: Leslie tries to continue her pitch to convince the woman to attend
jokes.punchline: The woman has already left mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: Classic comedic timing - Leslie's persistence meeting reality. Visual gag of person leaving during pitch.

jokes.text: Now, is this park gonna have a playground or, you know, maybe a pool for the kids? Oh, how old are your kids? No kids.
jokes.setup: Man enthusiastically asks about kid-friendly park features
jokes.punchline: Reveals he has no children
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - sets up expectation he's a parent, then subverts it. Creates awkwardness for Leslie and Ann.

jokes.text: Also, is the park gonna be at least 1,000 feet from my house? Because, you know, I really can't move again.
jokes.setup: Man continues asking about park specifics
jokes.punchline: Implies he's some kind of registered offender with distance restrictions
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that catches audience off-guard. The casual delivery makes it funnier. Explains why he has no kids but wants kid features.

jokes.text: He runs weird.
jokes.setup: Tom dramatically exits to work alone
jokes.punchline: April's blunt observation about his running style
jokes.explanation: Perfect April moment - cutting through drama with simple observation. Maximum economy with just three words.

jokes.text: Could the 4-year-old watch the 2-year-old?
jokes.setup: Woman explains she'd need a babysitter for her 4 and 2 year old
jokes.punchline: Leslie suggests the 4-year-old babysit the 2-year-old
jokes.explanation: Leslie's desperation leads to inappropriate suggestion. Shows her single-minded focus on getting attendance over practical concerns.

jokes.text: Are you gonna commit to coming to this meeting tomorrow, or are you gonna be a bitch?
jokes.setup: Tom's smooth-talking sales pitch to Mickey
jokes.punchline: Ends with calling him a bitch if he doesn't commit
jokes.explanation: Tom's aggressive sales tactic shows his character. Good escalation from smooth talk to insult. Unexpected directness.

jokes.text: Let's blow on each other's faces.
jokes.setup: April and Andy are hot and tired from canvassing
jokes.punchline: April's bizarre solution to cooling down
jokes.explanation: Perfectly weird April solution delivered deadpan. Unexpected alternative to normal cooling methods. High quotability factor.

jokes.text: Yeah, don't worry about it, I made it all up.
jokes.setup: Ann questions Leslie's survey about nuclear waste storage
jokes.punchline: Leslie casually admits she fabricated the entire survey
jokes.explanation: Leslie's casual admission of dishonesty shows her ends-justify-means mentality. Good character moment.

jokes.text: Survey. We surveyed crystal meth users.
jokes.setup: Resident asks how Leslie would know what meth users think about parks
jokes.punchline: Leslie doubles down claiming they actually surveyed meth users
jokes.explanation: Leslie's commitment to her fake survey escalates absurdly. The specificity of 'crystal meth users' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You don't care about your kids if you don't support this park!
jokes.setup: Leslie's frustration with a park opponent who has kids
jokes.punchline: Accuses the parent of not caring about their children
jokes.explanation: Leslie's desperation leads to inappropriate guilt trip. Classic cringe comedy - character making bad situation worse.

jokes.text: Oh, it's next month, forget about it. Oh, it's tomorrow night.
jokes.setup: Angry parent asks when the meeting is
jokes.punchline: Leslie tries to discourage attendance, then immediately gives correct info
jokes.explanation: Leslie's panic leads to contradictory information. Shows her internal conflict between honesty and self-preservation.

jokes.text: Am I talking to the two sexiest thighs in landscaping?
jokes.setup: Tom approaches potential contractors
jokes.punchline: Bizarre pickup line about landscaping thighs
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Tom line - inappropriate, specific, and delivered with confidence. 'Sexiest thighs in landscaping' is memorably weird.

jokes.text: He's our symbol. We'll wheel him around the neighborhood to get sympathy. He's a cute FDR.
jokes.setup: Leslie realizes they need Andy as their injured victim for sympathy
jokes.punchline: Compares wheelchair-bound campaigning to FDR
jokes.explanation: Dark but clever political strategy. 'Cute FDR' is an inspired comparison. Shows Leslie's ruthless campaign instincts.

jokes.text: There's a new wind blowing in government and I don't like it.
jokes.setup: Ron discusses the federal money and new programs
jokes.punchline: He's horrified by government actually doing things
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson moment - government employee who hates government activity. Character-driven irony.

jokes.text: Hey, Vlad! Look at these rocks! Let's pretend they're potatoes! Nikolai! Do you want to swim in the dirt?
jokes.setup: Leslie compares America to Russia regarding parks
jokes.punchline: Absurd Russian park activities with rocks as potatoes
jokes.explanation: Leslie's patriotic fervor leads to absurd Russian stereotypes. The specificity of activities makes it memorable.

jokes.text: President Knope, this park is awesome. Now we understand why you are the first female President of the United States.
jokes.setup: Leslie fantasizes about visiting the park with White House staff
jokes.punchline: Her park somehow led to becoming first female president
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie grandiosity - connecting small local project to presidential aspirations. Shows her ambitious delusions.

jokes.text: Only for one to 90 days.
jokes.setup: Leslie wants to postpone the town meeting
jokes.punchline: The postponement range is absurdly broad
jokes.explanation: Leslie's attempt to sound reasonable while being completely vague. The range from one day to three months is absurd.

jokes.text: Don's cement, It's the best you can get, Don's
jokes.setup: Tom offers to create a jingle for Don's cement company
jokes.punchline: The jingle is terrible and doesn't rhyme properly
jokes.explanation: Tom's misplaced confidence in his creative abilities. The forced rhyme and awkward meter make it appropriately bad.

jokes.text: Can you do that? Why? Please, April! This is serious! Can you do that? Probably.
jokes.setup: Leslie desperately needs April to support the park if called on
jokes.punchline: April's uncommitted 'probably' to Leslie's urgent request
jokes.explanation: Perfect April response - minimal commitment to maximum request. Contrast between Leslie's intensity and April's indifference.

jokes.text: This is gonna be a train wreck.
jokes.setup: The town meeting is about to begin
jokes.punchline: Mark's blunt assessment of what's coming
jokes.explanation: Mark as voice of reason/audience surrogate. Simple but effective foreshadowing of disaster to come.

jokes.text: None of them were able to make it tonight, unfortunately. But they totally exist. I did not make them up.
jokes.setup: Leslie claims many people support the park idea
jokes.punchline: Defensive insistence that these supporters are real
jokes.explanation: Leslie's desperation showing through defensive overexplanation. The 'I did not make them up' makes it obvious she did.

jokes.text: It's a rectangular lot. It's got excellent drainage.
jokes.setup: Mark is introduced as knowing everything and being amazing
jokes.punchline: His expertise amounts to basic geometric and drainage information
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Leslie's overselling of Mark. The mundane details contrast with her buildup.

jokes.text: Lawrence lives with his grandma. Which is pretty awesome. He takes care of her, I guess. But whatever, he's a douchebag.
jokes.setup: Andy tries to defend Lawrence living with his grandmother
jokes.punchline: Immediately calls him a douchebag anyway
jokes.explanation: Andy's attempt at fairness immediately abandoned. Good escalation from understanding to insult.

jokes.text: I'm in a band. It's called Just the Tip.
jokes.setup: Andy admits to playing loud music
jokes.punchline: His band has an inappropriate sexual innuendo name
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy obliviousness to inappropriate band name. High quotability due to memorable innuendo.

jokes.text: I'm oddly attracted to Kate Spivack.
jokes.setup: Kate has been aggressively opposing their park
jokes.punchline: Tom finds her opposition attractive
jokes.explanation: Tom's inappropriate attraction to aggressive opposition. Shows his character's misplaced priorities.

jokes.text: twisted him to death
jokes.setup: Leslie tells Pawnee's founding story about Reverend Luther Howell
jokes.punchline: The founder was 'twisted to death' by Native Americans
jokes.explanation: Unexpectedly violent and specific method of death in civic history. Dark humor in cheerful presentation.

jokes.text: Filibuster! Boom! They can't touch you if you talk forever.
jokes.setup: Leslie realizes she's talking too long
jokes.punchline: She consciously adopts filibuster strategy
jokes.explanation: Leslie's political knowledge deployed defensively. Self-aware comedy about her rambling.

jokes.text: blink our feelings to each other, rather than use words
jokes.setup: Leslie describes future Pawnee with space taxis and watch communication
jokes.punchline: Communication via blinking feelings
jokes.explanation: Weird future technology concept. Leslie's desperate rambling produces absurd sci-fi scenario.

jokes.text: Hi. Uh, I'm a youth in the community and I think a new park is a great idea. And I fully support it, as would the rest of the youth in the community.
jokes.setup: Leslie calls on April to support the park
jokes.punchline: April's robotic, unconvincing 'youth spokesperson' performance
jokes.explanation: April's deadpan delivery makes the planted support obvious. Perfect contrast to her usual disinterest.

jokes.text: Hey! I recognize her. That kid came to my door yesterday.
jokes.setup: April delivers her planted support speech
jokes.punchline: Resident recognizes her from canvassing, exposing the plant
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that exposes Leslie's desperate scheme. Good structure connecting earlier scenes to this disaster.

jokes.text: You suck. Hear that? He called me 'park lady.'
jokes.setup: Resident insults Leslie as they leave
jokes.punchline: Leslie focuses on the 'park lady' title as positive
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - finding positive in pure negativity. Shows her relentless optimism and identity formation. Excellent character integration and economy.

jokes.text: You know, normally, I don't agree with Leslie about anything, but this book is awesome.
jokes.setup: Mark has been skeptical of Leslie throughout
jokes.punchline: He's reading The Phantom Tollbooth from her filibuster
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to Leslie's desperate filibuster. Mark's character consistency with rare approval of Leslie idea.
